reason: The paper text itself never says which importance type Ranger/Boruta used ("relative importance obtained from the feature importance algorithm"), so the first-pass screen guessed "Ranger default = impurity." Checking the paper's own linked analysis code (github.com/slhogle/cyano-sidero-ocean, 07_global_metagenome_models/bin/fig4_random_forest.Rmd) shows the ranger engine is explicitly set with importance = "permutation" for the tuning and final models, and the PC ranking used for Fig 4/5 (and for the dFe claim) comes from step_select_boruta's Boruta object (boruta_obj$ImpHistory), which by default calls Boruta's getImpRfZ adapter - confirmed from the Boruta package source (importance.R) to run ranger with importance="permutation" (normalized permutation importance), not "impurity"/Gini. So while the magnitude/ranking of importance (dFe's high weighted-variance rank despite weak linear r) is indeed central to the paper's interaction/nonlinearity narrative, it is not impurity/MDI importance at all, so the impurity-magnification bias mechanism does not apply here. Corroboration of the specific ranking (beyond overall test R2=0.93) is thin, but that is moot since criterion (1) already fails.
